subject: Is the career advice you’re following actually wrong?
body:
A senior engineer explains why job-hopping and interviewing matter more than comfort.

View in browser (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsFKq87q_s0W29S0VY5ksKZWW2bnPc75NxQfyN8mSy5n5m_5PW7Y9pgv6lZ3lCVHSYy255gLvRW1dZHgn8tJmP6VZJlV47S3X33W899zyg2WjC1xN5Fg3QMQ2w0PN1H0xD8m7XnGW40bDWD4BWc1yV5MRHz1Dn8phW4HHBGz90kSyDW6kdyzF8cb1Y4W35LrYr580QpZW1c8cNf5mSgJ0W781CK-7B0l5kW4TSXzg784pWQVst9Cw220D-XW1N0-hj7YjgzyW599tq88T9jh-W5ZtbbK2zB5YMVWTp_p1cmt1vW87wVPk4JVwXWW2GvPb770lrRFW7l63xz1mMHJsW8jqvhM2NJr4yW6dcQ_h4sxhpkW6GyTcY50wM-CW93Lj0j2l44nrW9hx4R491bGxGN174hQp1tZTWW1c2Xvr8X1llpW6zmYW63mj3mbN5nmsT2WXslFW7TLXNt5q-HpZW4gZzMx49mBSRVggRm33p1K6pW6bMr7L6cpdrqW7CRWQn8l1pJjW1fFFZ963pklcN3BdfCnpQ9ThW1pXqZV8XQKTpVr89H28KgN_mW2CB2wd3T84J1W11DM2l4P2R6Tf4GgD0x04 )

Job-hopping and Interviewing

Денис,

Time for another guest newsletter!

This week, I’m excited to introduce Brian Jenney - a software engineer and engineering leader who broke into tech the hard way and isn’t shy about calling out bad career advice.

Brian didn’t come from a traditional CS background, and his perspective shows. His writing cuts through the feel-good LinkedIn fluff and gets straight to what actually works, especially for career-changers, juniors, and anyone without a perfect resume.

Today, he’s sharing his “forbidden” career advice for software developers: why likability beats perfection early on, why interviewing matters more than you think, and why playing it safe can be the riskiest move of all.

No sugarcoating. Just real talk.

Now over to Brian!

Before you go: your available offers on Educative subscriptions and upgrades are waiting. Your skills are your safety ned — keep weaving it.

View Offers → (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsFKq87q_s0W29S0VY5ksKZWW2bnPc75NxQfyN8mSy5H3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3pGW1fR03D1KNbGcW7d4lJd946Lm7W67rg3W5SCwrtW7yRndL3VTXrTN5BwK5Bq_11_W7lxCsz7RQn78W42NQVJ1zydmZW68QVY37QBMmlVlnxCM6RBZKQW19jqsf4jDCBQW4l9XZm943KVNW6_YCJ356NFb-W5WBNK02Tb-z3W8Z0Sph1lcTk7W1sYFGJ774HgsVx_5m_3WHP3VW94s2L87WK6kqW3vsVBZ29tL7SW491XTn84qWH2W22j3DC3mwBBMW956Yw_2ZfsmKW6nLKp_2qCjtWW6XDK-Y1XsGxbN2W4VkLcsZnSW3RgY6F8LHv6TW22Vbbt77sYK7W5xLHHK130G4GW2bwvLM1zwj64f3-cmWd04 )

Forbidden Career Advice For Software Developers

(the stuff no one tells you)

I want to offer you some politically incorrect career advice.

The stuff that took me years to figure out that the gurus on LinkedIn won’t ever tell you, because - and listen closely here:

They don’t get it.

They’re writing for the college grads trying to break into Google. Not the father of a newborn trying to switch careers.

I came into tech as an extreme outsider. It wasn’t just that I didn’t have a CS degree. A year before I landed my first coding job I had an intervention (yes, that kind of intervention). I didn’t even own a computer as an adult. Between me and you, I don’t like video games either.

Don’t judge me.

Fast forward 7 years from that intervention, and I’d become an engineering manager and landed multiple roles at startups in the SF Bay Area.

Unfortunately, even though you might be smarter than I, you’re probably trying to plan your career using an outdated playbook.

Let’s get into it.

The Road to Hell Is Paved With Good Intentions

Most career advice you read online is nicely packaged fluff that sounds amazing, but is usually written by people who either:

Never really worked as developers (this is shockingly common)

Work in massive tech orgs with clear structure

Have a very traditional background (CS degree and/or coding in teen years)

This doesn’t make their advice bad at all. It’s just unlikely to work for career changers and those of us outside Big Tech.

I’ve made too many mistakes over the years to count and learned a hell of a lot by going from crime to coding in my 30s.

Here’s what I wish someone had told me:

Be Likeable & Be Undeniable

(It’s ok to be a personality hire first… and a technical hire second)

At a junior level, you’re not competing technically with mid-level and senior engineers. You’re competing on communication, clarity, adaptability, and energy.

In other words, it is completely fine to be a “personality hire.” In fact, that’s what you should be striving for.

Being a personality hire basically means you have potential and you’re “likeable.”

What people actually mean by “likeable” is:

Can you explain your thinking clearly?

Can you accept feedback without shutting down?

Can you communicate technical decisions like a human being?

Do you make the room calmer and easier to work in?

Too many people who are genuinely nice completely fall apart when the interview starts. They clam up. They ramble. They get defensive.

This kills offers faster than any technical weakness ever will.

If you want to improve this fast:

Record yourself explaining a technical concept. Watching it back will hurt your feelings - but it will fix your communication.

Put yourself on camera often. Nervous on video === nervous in interviews.

Learn the language of developers. Read 1–2 books about software (Clean Code and The Phoenix Project are two of my favorites). Learn common design patterns. If you come from a non-traditional path, this is how you close the credibility gap.

Likability can be enough when you first start. Then you must be good at the thing.

Be undeniable in your talent, not only for your career but for yourself. Prove to yourself this isn’t a fluke.

That means taking the assignments no one wants, learning the boring systems no one touches, and becoming the person who quietly unblocks the rest of the team.

Your first job is for validation and learning, not coasting.

The Highest Paying Skill You Don’t Have Yet

(Learning how to interview > learning how to code)

This is an unfair reality in tech:

Interviewing is the single highest-leverage skill you will ever develop as a software engineer.

Interviewing creates optionality.

• It gives you confidence.

• It makes you layoff-resilient.

• It increases your income more directly than anything else.

And the sooner you accept this, the faster you’ll rise.

Your pass rate will be low. That’s normal. Interviewing is a volume game.

The reason I got good at interviews wasn’t because I was “naturally talented”; it was because I did dozens of mock interviews and studied relentlessly.

I built common data structures from scratch and learned when to apply them. Pattern-based prep like Grokking the Coding Interview (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsFKq87q_s0W29S0VY5ksKZWW2bnPc75NxQfyN8mSy5-3prCCW95jsWP6lZ3lzW5Tl0K17hG2Z_VrLtmy3z82X7W6JYlpN6NN-ZmW5GR5sq68Vt0fVdfNSr4qcckFW4SdnZ51CxGvsW6kzrZD5xqX9tW1qNqV36CLtJFW27sj_t31SvWXW2y4dN27kt5Y4W8F5TCl5X4wVMW5Z-0ww1K2BB9W1VC_Ff5CDh85N48HdvR1zRWBW45P4r19bMRWlW4DpW1w6PbdjQW20nMry3ZBjM3W456dtd3vqFSpW4P--ym8zM6P5MfN241vK39YV4TnqM6yrbv1MTKPC36N8v5W3cVF8s3k-nmKN7L7PSxDY5PBVnm5dC6kQCD0W3H8k0k5Y7wgfW8Tqs3Q1-xT4DW7gc9Nn3cHclTW5NFF8j27LyfXW5v8k8W2lzwVMf7hFKG604 )  is great for learning how to recognise and solve the kinds of problems that show up again and again in interviews.

Learning is hard, but mock interviews are what make the biggest difference. This is what prepares you for “game time.”

I’ve personally seen people earn $10k… $20k… even $50k more simply by being slightly better at interviewing.

Personally, I increased my salary 400% by getting better at interviewing. Seriously.

Small improvements create massive opportunities.

Here’s something you’re not going to like:

You should be interviewing every six months, even when you have a job.

That’s how you know:

• If you’re underpaid

• If you’re overpaid

• If your skills translate in today’s market

• If you’re actually “safe” in a downturn

• If you still have options when you need them

The best time to interview is when you already have a job.

The worst time is after you’ve been laid off.

And as you move past junior roles, interviews shift from “write this function” to “design a system.” Resources like Grokking the System Design Interview (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsFKq87q_s0W29S0VY5ksKZWW2bnPc75NxQfyN8mSy3W5m_5PW50kH_H6lZ3mZW7TJ7PW3pqqcvW4yC4xr8-TMWlW4yTzcj2g21JhW2qNZN22QVvkcW5yVvLM7xZCxyW66Q0LJ2wyGHwW7_FMWd452GF8V_bHNp5PJnYYW97t4Hh51HdxZN6Y_xzFcKDpxW1QBsTM74k8zKN97tV3Shbh5lW21jbxs6S7K6gW2Jx9DY7b5MVnW1-jY-V8J9_T-W7HGLZN3zQhzdW8m_7CD90mQbsW4qxWnk9j81tMW1zYVrf8DKCzvW8khVXV7HCXTQW2Y7zVj2rx5pCN8nQxqXqzbBRW98H8Vt4PyRv6N1mhDQ1PJ4GnW81TRnh3MJYS0W4GcxwM5zXyVSW2BRrLq5NT_MJW7nFCrf5r1CKCW7Cf6Jf83Y20xVtcNsd4wTV1RW5DWM7f4rQCyDW4KwJ2L4LH-qxdZd11q04 )  are excellent for learning how to think through scalable architectures and tradeoffs. This is the stuff that unlocks higher-level roles.

Learn More → (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsFKq87q_s0W29S0VY5ksKZWW2bnPc75NxQfyN8mSy5H3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3pGW1fR03D1KNbGcW7d4lJd946Lm7W67rg3W5SCwrtW7yRndL3VTXrTN5BwK5Bq_11_W7lxCsz7RQn78W42NQVJ1zydmZW68QVY37QBMmlVlnxCM6RBZKQW19jqsf4jDCBQW4l9XZm943KVNW6_YCJ356NFb-W5WBNK02Tb-z3W8Z0Sph1lcTk7W1sYFGJ774HgsVx_5m_3WHP3VW94s2L87WK6kqW3vsVBZ29tL7SW491XTn84qWH2W22j3DC3mwBBMW956Yw_2ZfsmKW6nLKp_2qCjtWW6XDK-Y1XsGxbN2W4VkLcsZnSW3RgY6F8LHv6TW22Vbbt77sYK7W5xLHHK130G4GW2bwvLM1zwj64f3-cmWd04 )

Become an Influencer

(Literally and figuratively)

Yes, I mean this literally — and figuratively.

I can’t believe I’m writing this, but… I became a tech influencer.

I love to write, and I also have a business to run. I didn’t expect it, but it opened up ridiculous amounts of opportunity: selling products, building my business, monetizing my content, and becoming highly discoverable on LinkedIn.

It also has downsides. Some recruiters have ghosted me the second they saw I owned a business or because of things I wrote.

Writing online has been 99% upside, however. Consider putting yourself out there and sharing what you’re learning. Be anti-cringe.

But here’s the part you need to understand:

You don’t have to become a social media creator.

But you should aim to have influence wherever you work.

Influence starts small:

Ask questions in meetings

Volunteer to run something small, like an epic

Shadow the people doing the critical work

Emulate the best people on the team - copy their habits and style

Improve processes, culture, documentation, and onboarding if you’re more junior

Lead postmortems or stand-ups

This is how you build actual leadership practice without the title.

This is how you become someone others trust.

Influence first.

Title later.

And once you have influence, loyalty becomes a tool, not an obligation.

Loyalty Is Overrated

(And resume-driven development is ok)

Early in your career, job-hop. It’s the fastest way to lift your salary trajectory.

But eventually you need to stay somewhere long enough to see how your decisions play out. That’s how you grow.

It’s also much easier to get promoted from within and then take that title with you to the next job.

Here’s my personal rule:

You can leave after you’ve delivered something impactful enough to write about.

A measurable outcome

A big improvement

A cultural shift

A feature with real results

Get two of those, and you can leave - even if it’s only been 9 months.

Impact is impact.

Resume-driven development isn’t necessarily bad. In fact, it’s a great way to guide your decisions toward the kinds of projects that grow your career. If you do this ethically, it’s a win for you and the team.

Lastly, It’s OK to Be Average

(Why chase what you don’t want?)

I wrote this article assuming fast career trajectory and increased salary are goals of yours.

The steps I suggested will be stressful. You will need to decide if it’s worth it.

Being average was my original goal when I started coding - and honestly, that’s not a terrible goal.

Average developers keep the machine running. They build the features, fix the bugs, and don’t necessarily want to play politics or build side projects on the weekends.

Perhaps your passion is outside coding.

However, if you do want to rise faster, have more optionality, and make more money, then playing it safe will not work.

In this case, playing it “safe” is the riskiest thing you can do.

What works in real life and what sounds good in a LinkedIn post are often violently opposed.

Good luck in whatever path you decide to take.

Happy building!

Brian Jenney

Senior Software Engineer and Owner of Parsity (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWsFKq87q_s0W29S0VY5ksKZWW2bnPc75NxQfyN8mSy5n3prCCW7Y8-PT6lZ3nJW2JKZjp8XK5f2W7vlz_H3Jbm5PW6dq_F43cYP6SW13gQlj45JHdKW8QhFMV4KXYMyW613T-b3VB9QvN520503HDhLrW6_LwHj8GrnBMVpZ4sZ7hpHSWN72N3Qnd59nrW1PBpcD14GFd2W6xcW0311NQtWW6x8FRw1-G_1wW7Ls0475PNFSRW2b2zxP5Pjw_kW3y4P5g1sWXzBW6NfZNX2whGHmW8nq9yC6B4hrVW2hqBsv1vt94ZVpxwKz2m_2g8W8tbdcp4l22HgN1wlN7QcTB-YW2gCmpY3bPhGdW83fyHm56B9MbVRklMR1qhYK9W5qxx911XGZf4f91hv9v04 )
